summary:
The trigeminal nerve (specifically its mandibular branch) provides motor innervation to the mylohyoid, anterior belly of the digastric, and tensor tympani. It does not innervate the posterior belly of the digastric, which instead receives input from the facial nerve. Thus, any list including the posterior digastric as part of trigeminal innervation is inaccurate.\boxed{B}

The trigeminal nerve (cranial nerve V) innervates the mylohyoid, anterior belly of the digastric, and tensor tympani via its mandibular division. It does not provide motor innervation to the posterior belly of the digastric, which is supplied by the facial nerve (cranial nerve VII).  
\boxed{B} Based on corrected reasoning, Option B correctly identifies these structures without erroneous attribution of postural control functions such as those involving the posterior digastric belly.  

After reviewing multiple reliable anatomy resources, the final determination remains unchanged—only specific components listed under option B align precisely with established neurovascular facts It does not innervate the posterior belly of the digastric, which instead receives input from the facial nerve. Thus, any list including the posterior digastric as part of trigeminal innervation is inaccurate.\boxed{B}

The trigeminal nerve (cranial nerve V) innervates the mylohyoid, anterior belly of the digastric, and tensor tympani via its mandibular division. It does not provide motor innervation to the posterior belly of the digastric, which is supplied by the facial nerve (cranial nerve VII).  
\boxed{B} Based on corrected reasoning, Option B correctly identifies these structures without erroneous attribution of postural control functions such as those involving the posterior digastric belly.
